title: Burr–Erdős Problem on 2-Ramsey Complete Sequences (gap problem)
statement_text: Can the gap between the upper bound $|A \cap [n]| \leq C\log^3 n$ for the sparsest entirely 2-Ramsey complete sequence and the lower bound showing no 2-Ramsey complete sequence satisfies $|A \cap [n]| \leq c\log^2 n$ for all sufficiently large $n$ be narrowed?
context_text: Burr and Erdős constructed an entirely 2-Ramsey complete sequence with $|A \cap [n]| \leq C\log^3 n$ for all $n$, and showed there is a constant $c > 0$ for which no 2-Ramsey complete sequence satisfies $|A \cap [n]| \leq c\log^2 n$ for all sufficiently large $n$. They asked whether this gap could be narrowed; Erdős later offered $100 for an improvement.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.1 resolves the problem completely, showing that the sparsest $r$-Ramsey complete sequence has $|A \cap [n]| = \Theta(r\log^2 n)$, hence the correct order for $r=2$ is $\Theta(\log^2 n)$.
notes: PDF source — one of three Burr–Erdős problems; Erdős offered $100 prize. Math reconstructed from garbled PDF.

title: Burr–Erdős Problem on r-Ramsey Complete Sequences (r ≥ 3)
statement_text: For $r \geq 3$, does there exist an $r$-Ramsey complete sequence $A$ with $|A \cap [n]| = n^{o(1)}$? More generally, find any non-trivial upper bound on the density of the sparsest $r$-Ramsey complete sequence.
context_text: Burr and Erdős showed that for $r \geq 3$ there is no $r$-Ramsey complete sequence with $|A \cap [n]| \leq c\log^2 n$, but were unable to construct an $r$-Ramsey complete sequence with $|A \cap [n]| = n^{o(1)}$. Given the lack of progress, Erdős later offered $250 for any non-trivial result.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.1 resolves this by constructing, for every $r \geq 2$, an $r$-Ramsey complete sequence with $|A \cap [n]| \leq Cr\log^2 n$, and showing this is tight up to an absolute constant factor.
notes: PDF source — one of three Burr–Erdős problems; Erdős offered $250 prize. Math reconstructed from garbled PDF.

title: Burr–Erdős Question on Ramsey Complete Polynomial Sequences
context_text: After Graham characterized all complete real polynomial sequences, Burr and Erdős asked which such sequences are $r$-Ramsey complete. Burr reportedly proved (unpublished) that the sequence of $k$th powers is $r$-Ramsey complete for all $r \geq 2$.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.2 answers this completely: for any degree-$k$ polynomial $P$ for which $(P(m))_{m\geq 1}$ is complete and any $r \geq 2$, there is an $r$-Ramsey complete subsequence $A \subseteq (P(m))_{m\geq 1}$ with $|A \cap [n]| \leq C(k)r\log^2 n$.
notes: PDF source — identified as the third of the three Burr–Erdős problems referenced in the abstract (combined $350 prize).

title: Alon–Erdős Conjecture on f(n)
statement_text: The upper bound $f(n) \leq \frac{c_2 n^{1/3}(\log\log n)^{1/3}}{(\log n)^{1/3}}$ is closer to the true order of magnitude of $f(n)$ than the lower bound $f(n) \geq \frac{c_1 n^{1/3}}{\log^{4/3} n}$, where $f(n)$ is the minimum number of colors needed to color the positive integers less than $n$ so that $n$ cannot be written as a monochromatic sum of distinct integers.
context_text: After Erdős asked whether $f(n) = n^{1/3-o(1)}$, Alon and Erdős proved the two-sided bound $\frac{c_1 n^{1/3}}{\log^{4/3} n} \leq f(n) \leq \frac{c_2 n^{1/3}(\log\log n)^{1/3}}{(\log n)^{1/3}}$ and added that they suspect the upper bound is closer to the truth. Vu later refined the lower bound to $f(n) \geq \frac{c_1 n^{1/3}}{\log n}$.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.5 confirms the conjecture by determining $f(n) = \Theta\!\left(\frac{n^{1/3}(n/\varphi(n))}{(\log n)^{1/3}(\log\log n)^{2/3}}\right)$, showing the upper bound order is essentially correct and the lower bound is not sharp.
notes: PDF source — stated informally as a suspicion by Alon and Erdős; math reconstructed from garbled PDF.

title: Erdős–Graham Extremal Function Problem
statement_text: Determine the exact value of the extremal function $f$ introduced by Erdős and Graham giving the maximum size of a subset of $[n]$ that avoids a given subset sum.
context_text: Erdős and Graham introduced an extremal function studying the maximum size of a set of integers in $[n]$ that avoids a particular prescribed subset sum; also studied by Alon, Erdős, and Graham. The exact determination of this function was open.
paper_contribution: The paper determines the extremal function exactly.
notes: PDF source — only briefly mentioned in abstract and introduction; full statement not visible in the provided text excerpt. Attributed also to Alon, Erdős, and Graham [1, 18].

title: Question on Homogeneous Strengthening of Szemerédi–Vu Theorem
statement_text: Is there a homogeneous version of the Szemerédi–Vu theorem, i.e., does the conclusion that $\Sigma(A)$ contains a long arithmetic progression hold under the additional requirement that the progression is witnessed monochromatically (or homogeneously) with respect to a coloring of $A$?
context_text: Szemerédi and Vu proved that any subset $A$ of $[n]$ with $|A| \geq C\sqrt{n}$ that meets every infinite arithmetic progression contains a long arithmetic progression in $\Sigma(A)$. A homogeneous strengthening of this result was asked for by Erdős and Sárközy, by Sárközy, and independently by Tran, Vu, and Wood.
paper_contribution: The paper proves a homogeneous strengthening of the Szemerédi–Vu theorem, answering the question reiterated by multiple groups of authors.
notes: PDF source — question attributed to multiple groups: Erdős–Sárközy [22], Sárközy [36], and Tran–Vu–Wood [42]; precise statement not fully visible in the provided text excerpt.
